findings.title: Isle of Man sanctions legislation amendments effective May 13, 2026; FSA questionnaire due May 22, 2026
findings.summary: Sanctions legislation amendments take effect May 13, 2026, introducing end-user controls requiring export licences for sanctioned goods at risk of diversion to a sanctioned jurisdiction. The IoM FSA published proliferation financing guidance on May 7, 2026. A mandatory sanctions and proliferation financing questionnaire for financial firms closes May 22, 2026.
findings.client_impact: Any client with Isle of Man–based entities, trust company businesses, or investment structures must verify their sanctions screening policies comply with the new end-user controls before May 13. IoM-regulated fiduciaries or fund service providers must also submit the FSA's sanctions questionnaire by May 22 or face regulatory sanction.
findings.urgency_reason: Two hard deadlines in the next 14 days: amended law effective May 13 (5 days) and mandatory FSA questionnaire due May 22 (14 days). Non-response to the questionnaire is a direct compliance failure for IoM-regulated entities.
findings.source_name: Isle of Man Government — Sanctions Amendments from May 13, 2026

findings.title: ADGM FSRA consultation on enhanced AML rules closes May 14, 2026; mandatory high-risk classifications proposed
findings.summary: ADGM's FSRA consultation on tightened AML rules aligned with updated UAE AML laws and FATF standards closes May 14, 2026. Key proposals: mandatory high-risk classifications for foreign PEPs, high-risk jurisdictions, and unhosted wallet exposure; enhanced senior management accountability for AML systems oversight.
findings.client_impact: Clients with ADGM-regulated entities, fund managers, or wealth vehicles in Abu Dhabi Global Market must act immediately if they wish to submit comments before the consultation closes — after May 14, final rules will be issued without further industry input. The proposed mandatory high-risk classifications will increase due diligence burden on structures involving foreign PEPs.
findings.urgency_reason: Consultation window closes May 14, 2026 — 6 days from today. This is the last opportunity to influence mandatory high-risk classification rules; once final rules are issued, compliance timelines are typically short.
findings.source_name: Enterprise Asset Management — UAE: DIFC and ADGM Regulatory Updates May 2026

findings.title: Bahamas AML/CFT/CPF Guidelines revised March 19, 2026; CFATF mutual evaluation onsite October 2026
findings.summary: The Central Bank of The Bahamas revised its AML/CFT/CPF Guidelines on March 19, 2026, incorporating FATF proliferation financing standards. The Bahamas' CFATF Fifth Round Mutual Evaluation onsite visit is scheduled October 19–30, 2026, creating regulatory pressure to tighten enforcement across all supervised institutions in the lead-up.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Bahamas-based banks, trusts, or investment structures should expect heightened KYC/AML scrutiny ahead of the October CFATF evaluation; financial institutions may impose stricter documentation requirements and enhanced due diligence on existing and new accounts in the coming months.
findings.urgency_reason: No immediate deadline, but the approaching CFATF evaluation (October 2026) typically triggers a period of elevated regulatory enforcement activity. Clients should verify that their Bahamas-based structures are fully documented now, before supervisory pressure intensifies.
findings.source_name: Central Bank of The Bahamas — AML/CFT/CPF Guidelines (Revised March 19, 2026)

findings.title: BCB bans eFX firms from using stablecoins/crypto to settle overseas remittances (effective October 1, 2026)
findings.summary: BCB Resolution No. 561 (April 30, 2026) prohibits eFX providers from using stablecoins (USDT, USDC) or crypto assets to settle cross-border payments with foreign counterparties. Payments must route through traditional FX operations or non-resident BRL accounts. Effective October 1, 2026; adaptation deadlines extend into 2027. Affects Wise, Nomad, Braza Bank, and similar platforms. eFX transaction cap remains $10,000.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian HNW clients or their family members using eFX platforms (Wise, Nomad) to move funds internationally will need to switch to traditional FX channels before October 1; any crypto-based remittance workflows, including those using stablecoins as a bridge, must be restructured by that date.
findings.urgency_reason: The effective date is October 1, 2026 — nearly five months away — giving time to migrate, but the operational change for clients using eFX platforms is significant and requires identifying compliant alternatives in advance.
findings.source_name: CoinDesk

findings.title: BCB VASP authorization framework active from February 2, 2026; existing operators must apply by October 30, 2026
findings.summary: BCB Resolutions No. 519–521 established a formal regulatory regime for Virtual Asset Service Providers. VASPs operating before February 2, 2026 must apply for BCB authorization by October 30, 2026. New entrants must apply before commencing operations. The BCB and CVM conduct joint inspections of institutions administering investment funds.
findings.client_impact: Clients who hold interests in or use Brazilian VASP platforms for crypto transactions should confirm their platforms have filed or intend to file for BCB authorization before October 30, 2026; unlicensed VASPs face shutdown, which could trap client assets or disrupt investment fund administration.
findings.urgency_reason: The October 30, 2026 deadline for existing VASPs is approximately six months away. Clients should audit their Brazilian crypto-adjacent service providers now to verify authorization applications are in progress.
findings.source_name: Global Legal Insights — Banking Laws and Regulations 2026: Brazil

findings.title: BVI Register of Beneficial Ownership open to legitimate interest access effective April 1, 2026
findings.summary: From April 1, 2026, third parties may access the BVI Register of Beneficial Ownership under a 'legitimate interest' regime where their purpose relates to investigating or detecting money laundering, terrorist financing, or proliferation financing, or where a connection to a convicted person exists. The access threshold is 25% ownership or control. Information is filed directly with the Registry via the VIRRGIN system; the moratorium on penalties expired March 31, 2026.
findings.client_impact: Clients using BVI holding companies or SPVs should be aware that their beneficial ownership information is now potentially accessible to law enforcement, regulators, and qualifying third parties; structures involving individuals with political exposure or connection to AML investigations face heightened disclosure risk.
findings.urgency_reason: The regime is now live and penalties are being enforced. This represents a permanent, ongoing privacy and reputational risk for BVI structures — clients holding sensitive assets through BVI vehicles should review whether the jurisdiction still meets their confidentiality requirements.
findings.source_name: Harneys — BVI Beneficial Ownership Updates

findings.title: Cayman Beneficial Ownership Transparency Act (2026 Revision) — DITC Portal registration deadline April 30, 2026 has passed
findings.summary: The Beneficial Ownership Transparency Act (2026 Revision), published January 29, 2026, strengthens beneficial ownership collection and maintenance requirements in line with evolving FATF standards. Cayman entities that became Financial Institutions in 2025 were required to register on the DITC Portal by April 30, 2026. That deadline has now passed.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Cayman entities that became FIs in 2025 and have not yet registered on the DITC Portal are now non-compliant and should rectify immediately; late registration may attract penalties and complicate annual regulatory filings.
findings.urgency_reason: The April 30, 2026 deadline passed 8 days ago. Any client in scope who has not registered is currently in breach; this is an immediate remediation issue rather than a future planning item.
findings.source_name: Stuarts Humphries — Cayman Compliance and Regulatory Deadlines 2026

findings.title: CRS 2.0 effective January 1, 2026 in Cayman Islands; DITC registration deadline April 30, 2026 has passed
findings.summary: Cayman Islands CRS 2.0 amendments took effect January 1, 2026, introducing enhanced due diligence and reporting data fields (joint account status, self-certification validity, account type). Certain procedural elements apply from 2027. The DITC Portal registration deadline of April 30, 2026 for eligible Cayman FIs has now passed.
findings.client_impact: Cayman funds and investment vehicles with reportable account holders must now comply with CRS 2.0 data collection requirements; any Cayman FI that missed the April 30 DITC registration deadline is non-compliant and risks reporting failures that could trigger penalties and OECD peer-review findings against the jurisdiction.
findings.urgency_reason: The April 30 DITC deadline passed 8 days ago. Clients with Cayman FIs should confirm registration status immediately; ongoing CRS 2.0 compliance is a live requirement with new data fields that may require system updates.
findings.source_name: Ogier — Cayman Islands Funds and Regulatory Update April 2026

findings.title: Cyprus comprehensive tax reform effective January 1, 2026 — CIT raised to 15%, DDD abolished, dividend WHT cut to 5%
findings.summary: Cyprus enacted comprehensive tax reform from January 1, 2026 for Pillar Two alignment: CIT raised from 12.5% to 15%; Deemed Dividend Distribution rule abolished; dividend withholding tax reduced to 5%; stamp duty abolished; extended loss carry-forward; defensive measures for blacklisted-jurisdiction transactions. Investment fund benefits (participation exemptions, IP Box, AIF efficiencies) continue.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Cyprus holding companies or funds should review their corporate structures: the abolition of DDD removes a previous tax trap on retained profits, the 5% dividend WHT is now attractive for profit repatriation, but the 15% CIT may increase the effective tax rate on entities previously relying on the 12.5% rate for tax planning.
findings.urgency_reason: The reform took effect January 1, 2026 and is now fully in force; although there is no immediate filing deadline, clients should have already restructured or reviewed Cyprus-based holding arrangements. Any structures not reviewed since year-end 2025 need attention before the 2026 tax year materialises in filings.
findings.source_name: Eurofast — Cyprus 2026 Tax Reform: What It Means for Investment Funds

findings.title: Florida SB 264 foreign buyer restrictions upheld by Eleventh Circuit and ongoing — affidavit required at every closing
findings.summary: Florida SB 264 (effective July 1, 2023) remains in full force restricting buyers from seven countries of concern (China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, Syria) from purchasing agricultural land or real estate within 10–20 miles of military installations or critical infrastructure. Chinese nationals face felony charges; other restricted nationals face misdemeanors. Every Florida real property buyer must sign a compliance affidavit at closing. The Eleventh Circuit upheld enforcement.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who are nationals of — or who hold assets through entities domiciled in — the seven listed countries must confirm their Florida real estate does not violate SB 264 proximity restrictions; every transaction requires a signed affidavit, and failure to disclose nationality exposes clients to criminal liability.
findings.urgency_reason: No new deadline — this is an ongoing enforcement obligation. The Eleventh Circuit ruling removes any remaining legal uncertainty, so clients with pending Florida acquisitions involving restricted-country connections should seek counsel before closing.
findings.source_name: South Florida Law — Foreign Buyer Restrictions in Florida Real Estate 2026

findings.title: Isle of Man Central Registry proposals to strengthen beneficial ownership framework (April 2026)
findings.summary: The Isle of Man Central Registry published proposals in April 2026 to update the definition of 'registrable beneficial owner' and change required disclosures when an entity has no registrable beneficial owner. The Financial Services (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill 2026 was introduced to Tynwald on March 31, 2026, with a FSA Feedback Statement published in April 2026.
findings.client_impact: Clients holding Isle of Man companies or structures should monitor these proposals, as an updated 'registrable beneficial owner' definition could bring previously exempt arrangements into scope; those relying on nominee or layered ownership structures in IoM should review whether they will remain compliant once the Bill is enacted.
findings.urgency_reason: The Bill is in active legislative progress through Tynwald; while no compliance deadline has been set yet, the FSA's feedback statement suggests final legislation is imminent. Early structural review is preferable to reactive remediation.
findings.source_name: Isle of Man FSA — Central Registry Proposals: April 2026

findings.title: Luxembourg Pillar Two GloBE Information Return due June 30, 2026; registration and filing launched
findings.summary: Luxembourg has launched Pillar Two GIR registration and XML filing procedures for calendar-year MNE groups ahead of the June 30, 2026 deadline. The GIR requires comprehensive disclosure of profits, taxes, and corporate structure across every constituent entity. The OECD released the Global Minimum Tax Implementation Toolkit on April 30, 2026. Luxembourg qualifies for the US side-by-side safe harbor for US-parented MNEs.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Luxembourg MNE group entities (typically those with consolidated annual revenues ≥ €750M) must ensure their Luxembourg subsidiary is registered and ready to file the GIR or that the group's designated filing entity covers Luxembourg; the XML format and multi-jurisdictional data aggregation are operationally complex and require early preparation.
findings.urgency_reason: The June 30, 2026 deadline is 53 days away — within the typical preparation window for a complex XML filing. MNE groups that have not yet engaged Luxembourg tax advisors to begin GIR preparation should do so now.
findings.source_name: EY Luxembourg — Pillar Two Registration and Return Filing

findings.title: OECD Pillar Two: first GloBE Information Return due June 30, 2026; Implementation Toolkit released April 30, 2026
findings.summary: The first GIR filings for calendar-year MNE groups are due June 30, 2026. The GIR is filed once per group and exchanged under Multilateral Competent Authority Agreements. The OECD Implementation Toolkit (April 30, 2026) provides simplified reporting guidance; simplification work is expected to conclude by June 2026. The US qualifies for the side-by-side safe harbor, exempting US-parented groups from IIR and UTPR in qualifying jurisdictions.
findings.client_impact: Any MNE group with global revenues ≥ €750M that includes HNW clients as controlling shareholders must ensure their group's Pillar Two compliance position and GIR filing obligations have been assessed across all constituent jurisdictions; the side-by-side safe harbor for US-parented groups significantly reduces the risk of double taxation for US clients with international subsidiaries.
findings.urgency_reason: June 30, 2026 is 53 days away. The GIR is a multi-jurisdiction XML filing requiring months of data gathering; groups without an active compliance workstream are at acute risk of missing the deadline.
findings.source_name: OECD — Global Anti-Base Erosion Model Rules (Pillar Two)

findings.title: Panama economic substance requirements — 15% gross income tax for non-compliant entities; Declaración Jurada due June 30, 2026
findings.summary: A legislative proposal before Panama's National Assembly would impose a 15% tax on gross income of international entities failing to satisfy substance or reporting requirements, in line with OECD Pillar Two. The Declaración Jurada de Requerimientos de Sustancia must be filed within six months of fiscal year-end (June 30, 2026 for December 31 year-ends). The measure forms part of Panama's effort to exit the EU List of Non-Cooperative Jurisdictions.
findings.client_impact: Clients holding international Panama entities (Sociedades Anónimas or foundations used as holding structures) must ensure substance requirements are documented and the Declaración Jurada is filed by June 30, 2026; failure risks a 15% gross income tax and continued blacklisting by the EU, which affects the use of these entities in European transactions.
findings.urgency_reason: The June 30, 2026 filing deadline is 53 days away and applies to all December fiscal-year-end Panama international entities; this requires substance documentation and legal engagement now to prepare the declaration in time.
findings.source_name: KPMG Panama — Proposal on Economic Substance Requirements

findings.title: DIFC opens Private Company (PC) regime to all global applicants (announced May 1, 2026)
findings.summary: DIFC is removing qualifying requirements tied to ownership, purpose, or geographic nexus for Private Company status, making the DIFC PC vehicle accessible to any global investor. Most PCs will need to appoint a DFSA-regulated corporate service provider for filings and compliance. This materially broadens the universe of potential DIFC holding company users.
findings.client_impact: This is a significant new structuring opportunity for HNW clients worldwide: a DIFC Private Company can now be established as an international holding vehicle regardless of the client's domicile or the nationality of underlying assets, with the credibility of a DFSA-regulated framework and access to UAE's extensive treaty network.
findings.urgency_reason: No compliance deadline — this is an opportunity, not an obligation. Clients currently evaluating offshore holding structures (Cayman, BVI, Luxembourg) should now include the DIFC PC as a competitive alternative following this regulatory change.
findings.source_name: Enterprise Asset Management — UAE: DIFC and ADGM Regulatory Updates May 2026

findings.title: US federal estate and gift tax exemption raised to $15 million per person for 2026
findings.summary: The federal estate and gift tax exemption increased to $15 million per person ($30 million combined for married couples) for 2026, with annual inflation adjustments from 2027. Annual gift tax exclusion: $19,000 per donee ($38,000 for couples). Estate tax rate remains 40%. For non-resident foreign nationals, the estate tax exemption remains $60,000 with FIRPTA applying on US real property dispositions. Iowa eliminated its inheritance tax in 2025.
findings.client_impact: US domiciliaries with estates below $15 million per person ($30 million per couple) should review whether existing irrevocable trust structures or lifetime gifting programs set up to address the previous lower exemption are still the optimal strategy; the increased exemption creates significant planning flexibility but the permanent nature of the change is not guaranteed beyond the current legislative cycle.
findings.urgency_reason: No immediate deadline, but the exemption increase creates a time-sensitive planning window: clients who made taxable gifts or established irrevocable trusts in 2024–2025 in anticipation of a lower exemption should model whether additional planning or restructuring is warranted under the new $15M threshold.
findings.source_name: Kiplinger — 2026 Estate Tax Exemption Amount

findings.title: Brazil UBO identification rules clarified and reinforced effective April 1, 2026
findings.summary: Updated Brazilian beneficial ownership regulations effective April 1, 2026 clarify UBO identification requirements, reinforce documentation of control mechanisms, and improve transparency for legal entities. The UBO threshold remains 25% ownership or voting rights. CNPJ-registered entities since January 2023 must provide UBO information within 30 days of registration, with failure resulting in CNPJ suspension.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Brazilian operating or holding entities must ensure their UBO records and control-mechanism documentation are current and filed within CNPJ requirements; the clarifications reduce ambiguity but do not materially change the 25% threshold already in use.
findings.urgency_reason: The rules are already in force as of April 1, 2026 and represent a clarification rather than a new obligation; background awareness for clients with Brazilian corporate structures, no immediate action required beyond verifying existing UBO filings are complete.
findings.source_name: VoveID Blog

findings.title: Cayman Tokenised Funds Acts 2026 in force March 24, 2026 — formal framework for digital equity tokens
findings.summary: The Mutual Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 and Private Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 commenced March 24, 2026, introducing a formal regulatory framework for tokenised investment funds. Tokenised funds are regulated identically to traditional funds; CIMA has supervisory powers over the underlying technology and digital token transactions. Issuance of digital equity or investment tokens by these funds is excluded from the VASP regime.
findings.client_impact: Clients managing or investing in Cayman funds considering tokenisation of equity interests now have a clear legal framework: tokenised funds carry the same CIMA regulatory status as traditional funds, providing institutional credibility while avoiding the separate VASP licensing burden.
findings.urgency_reason: The framework is already in force; this is background awareness for clients exploring tokenised fund structures or who already hold interests in Cayman funds that may convert to a tokenised format.
findings.source_name: Prokopiev Law

findings.title: GFSC AML/CFT Handbook updated — proliferation financing and enhanced due diligence tightened (2025–2026)
findings.summary: The GFSC revised its Handbook on Countering Financial Crime in 2025–2026 to address proliferation financing, enhanced scenario analysis, and updated national risk assessment findings. Changes include modifications to rules on pooled bank accounts and Chapter 12 amendments covering UN, UK, and Bailiwick sanctions. The update prepares Guernsey for upcoming MoneyVal assessments.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Guernsey-based trustees, fund administrators, or fiduciaries should expect their service providers to request updated KYC documentation, enhanced transaction monitoring disclosures, and potentially additional PEP or sanctions screening in connection with the handbook revisions.
findings.urgency_reason: Changes are already in effect; awareness item for clients with Guernsey structures so they can anticipate enhanced due diligence requests from Guernsey-based service providers.
findings.source_name: Ocorian — GFSC Handbook Updates

findings.title: CRS 2.0 effective January 1, 2026 in UK; EMIs in scope; separate CRS/FATCA filings required from January 2027
findings.summary: The UK adopted CRS 2.0 on January 1, 2026, adding reporting data fields (joint account status, self-certification validity, account type). E-money products and Electronic Money Institutions are explicitly within CRS scope for the first time. From January 1, 2027, CRS and FATCA must be submitted as separate filings; the combined schema is retired December 31, 2026.
findings.client_impact: Clients holding accounts at UK financial institutions — including e-money accounts — will see more detailed information reported to HMRC and onward to partner jurisdictions under CRS 2.0; the additional data fields increase the accuracy of automatic exchange, reducing the effectiveness of undisclosed offshore arrangements.
findings.urgency_reason: The UK CRS 2.0 reporting framework is live but the first reporting deadline under the new standard is not until 2027; this is background awareness for clients with UK bank or e-money accounts to understand their information is being reported with enhanced detail.
findings.source_name: MacroGlobal — HMRC CRS 2.0 Updates for 2026

findings.title: Control of Borrowing (Jersey) Amendment Order 2026 in force April 13, 2026 — COBO scope significantly reduced
findings.summary: The COBO Amendment Order came into effect April 13, 2026, materially reducing circumstances requiring regulatory consent for transactions in Jersey. This is the first phase of simplification before full COBO repeal in 2027, affecting law firms, fund services, trust company businesses, and investment businesses.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Jersey fund structures or SPVs will benefit from a reduced consent burden for certain transactions that previously required JFSC approval; this deregulatory step makes Jersey more operationally flexible for fund launches and corporate transactions pending the full 2027 repeal.
findings.urgency_reason: Simplification is in force and beneficial — no action required. Relevant for clients considering new Jersey structures or awaiting consent-triggered transactions that may now proceed without prior JFSC approval.
findings.source_name: Mondaq — Jersey COBO Reform: First Phase of Simplification Now in Force

findings.title: Trusts (Jersey) Amendment Law 2026 (Eighth Amendment) in force March 20, 2026
findings.summary: The Eighth Amendment to the Trusts (Jersey) Law 1984 came into force March 20, 2026. Key changes: power to terminate a trust cannot be exercised if other persons could become beneficiaries; secured creditors' claims outrank trustee liens; a sole trustee cannot resign if it would leave the trust without any trustees.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Jersey trusts should confirm with their trustees that governing documents are consistent with the amended law — in particular, that any termination provisions do not inadvertently conflict with the new restriction on termination where future beneficiaries exist.
findings.urgency_reason: The amendment is already in force and represents clarificatory rather than structural change; advisors managing Jersey trusts with complex beneficiary classes or pending trustee transitions should review for any practical impact.
findings.source_name: Walkers — Jersey Trust Law Update: Eighth Amendment Now in Force

findings.title: Luxembourg carried interest tax regime revamped, applicable from fiscal year 2026
findings.summary: Bill of law n° 8590 (adopted January 22, 2026) revamps Luxembourg's carried interest tax regime with no caps or time limitations. Carried interest linked to alternative investment fund participations held more than six months representing no more than 10% of fund capital is not taxable. Replaces the previous complex regime from fiscal year 2026.
findings.client_impact: Fund managers or investment professionals receiving carried interest through Luxembourg structures should review whether their arrangements qualify under the new regime's six-month hold and 10% share capital thresholds — those that do now benefit from a clean, uncapped exemption with no administrative complexity.
findings.urgency_reason: The new regime applies from fiscal year 2026 and is already in force; background awareness for fund professionals with Luxembourg carried interest arrangements. Those who relied on the old regime should confirm their structures satisfy the new requirements.
findings.source_name: Baker McKenzie — Luxembourg Carried Interest Reform Adopted

findings.title: Malta MFSA revised sponsors regime effective January 1, 2026 — public register launched
findings.summary: The MFSA's revised sponsors regime, applicable from January 1, 2026, establishes a public register of registered sponsors on the MFSA website, enhancing market transparency and sponsor accountability in Malta's capital markets framework.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Malta-domiciled funds or listed vehicles should confirm that their sponsor is registered on the MFSA's public register; engaging an unregistered sponsor would place a transaction at risk of regulatory challenge.
findings.urgency_reason: The regime is in force; straightforward background awareness for clients using Malta capital markets structures. Verify sponsor registration status as a routine due diligence step.
findings.source_name: Mondaq — MFSA Revised Sponsors Regime 2026

findings.title: Singapore Securities and Futures (Amendment) Bill 2026 passed May 7, 2026 — SGX-Nasdaq Global Listing Board framework
findings.summary: The SFA Amendment Bill passed its Second Reading May 7, 2026. It inserts a new Part 13A empowering MAS to facilitate a SGX-Nasdaq Global Listing Board (GLB) enabling simultaneous dual-listings. Issuers can distribute preliminary prospectuses to retail investors before final lodgement. New market misconduct provisions apply in the GLB context.
findings.client_impact: Clients with portfolio companies considering international capital markets listings should note that the Singapore GLB framework now enables simultaneous SGX and Nasdaq dual-listing — a new pathway for Asia-facing companies seeking access to both US and regional retail capital at the same time.
findings.urgency_reason: The Bill just passed — no compliance deadline applies. Background awareness for clients exploring equity capital markets structures; the dual-listing pathway is a new option that did not exist before this legislation.
findings.source_name: MAS — Second Reading Speech for Securities and Futures (Amendment) Bill 2026

findings.title: Switzerland FinIA amendment consultation closed February 2026 — new payment and crypto institution licences proposed
findings.summary: The Swiss Federal Council's consultation on FinIA amendments proposing two new licence categories (payment institutions and crypto institutions) closed February 6, 2026. If adopted, crypto-asset firms will be brought under formal FINMA supervision. Final legislation timeline is pending.
findings.client_impact: Clients with interests in Swiss crypto or payment technology businesses should monitor the FinIA legislative timeline; formal FINMA licensing would increase operating costs and compliance obligations but would also enhance institutional credibility for client-facing Swiss crypto firms.
findings.urgency_reason: Legislation is pending with no enacted deadline; background awareness for clients with Swiss crypto or fintech exposure.
findings.source_name: Pestalozzi Attorneys at Law — Regulatory Milestones in Swiss Financial Market Regulation

findings.title: UAE Federal Decree-Laws overhaul onshore capital markets framework effective January 1, 2026
findings.summary: Federal Decree-Laws effective January 1, 2026 repealed Federal Law No. 4 of 2000 and introduced a comprehensive statute-driven regime governing UAE onshore capital markets under the Capital Market Authority. This is the most significant UAE onshore securities law reform in over two decades.
findings.client_impact: Clients with onshore UAE investments, listed securities, or plans to access UAE capital markets through the mainland (rather than DIFC or ADGM) should review their arrangements under the new CMA framework, as conduct, disclosure, and market structure obligations have been materially updated.
findings.urgency_reason: The framework took effect January 1, 2026 and is already in operation; background awareness for clients with UAE onshore capital markets exposure.
findings.source_name: Cleary Gottlieb — UAE Capital Markets Overhaul 2026

findings.title: FinCEN BOI: US companies and US persons permanently exempt; foreign entities registered in the US remain in scope
findings.summary: FinCEN's interim final rule (March 26, 2025) permanently exempts all domestically formed US entities and their beneficial owners from BOI reporting. Only foreign entities registered to do business in a US State or tribal jurisdiction remain in scope. Foreign entities registered before March 26, 2025 had an April 25, 2025 deadline; new registrants have 30 days. Foreign entities need not report US persons as beneficial owners.
findings.client_impact: Non-US clients with foreign entities registered to do business in US states must confirm BOI reports were filed by the applicable deadline; failure means the entity is currently non-compliant and subject to FinCEN penalties, even though US domestic entities were exempted.
findings.urgency_reason: The US exemption and foreign-entity scope are settled law as of March 2025; this is background awareness — primarily a reminder that foreign-registered entities remain in scope and that any new foreign registrations in the US trigger a 30-day BOI filing obligation.
findings.source_name: FinCEN.gov — Beneficial Ownership Reporting Requirements Update
